ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for 'toasties' or 'toasted filled bread'; FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers toasted bread as a category; no specific filled-toastie code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps specifically to toasties; omitted to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 pieces of toasted bread (approximately 200 g), typically filled with ham, cheese, and butter or margarine, served as a light snack or meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sandwich, with meat and cheese on white bread (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food for local context
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input values (energy 225 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 12.5 g, carbohydrate 17.5 g, sodium 400 mg, saturated fat 6.0 g, trans fat 0.25 g, sugar 2.0 g, dietary fibre 1.5 g, calcium 100 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 30 mg) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for a ham-and-cheese toasted sandwich on white bread (USDA FDC ID 172004 and related entries). Values retained from input and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy composite sandwich entries. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrients not reported in input are set to -1 where no approved-source figure was available for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread toast
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 70 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread (GI 70, glucose reference). Toasties are made primarily from white bread; toasting white bread does not substantially alter GI (Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 35.0) / 100 = 24.5, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 17.5 × 2 = 35.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 400 mg/100 g (800 mg per 200 g serving) from cured ham, cheese, and bread, with negligible potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load of 800 mg per serving (33% of the 2400 mg daily limit); substituting fresh chicken or turkey for ham and using low-sodium bread and cheese can substantially reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base (GI 70) with 35 g carbohydrates per serving and only 3 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of 24.5 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish because the high-GI white bread delivers a glycemic load of 24.5 per serving, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; choosing wholemeal or multigrain bread and limiting to one piece can meaningfully reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g (12 g per serving) and trans fat at 0.25 g/100 g (0.5 g per serving) from butter/margarine and cheese, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish regularly due to its high saturated fat (12 g per serving, 60% of the 20 g daily limit) and measurable trans fat (0.5 g per serving), both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase atherosclerotic risk; replacing margarine with a trans-fat-free spread and reducing cheese quantity are the most impactful modifications.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory saturated fat (6.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and processed pork (ham) rich in arachidonic acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to its combination of saturated fat, trans fat, and processed pork, all of which promote inflammatory cytokine activity; substituting ham with fresh chicken or a plant-based protein and using a trans-fat-free spread are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Toasties (2 pieces) is a popular Western-style snack or light meal served widely in Singaporean cafés, kopitiams, and fast-food outlets. The dish consists of two slices of white bread that are toasted or grilled until golden and crisp, typically filled with a savoury combination of ham (cured pork), processed cheese, and butter or margarine. The result is a warm, crispy-on-the-outside, soft-on-the-inside sandwich with a salty, slightly fatty flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 225 kcal, 10 g protein, 12.5 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated fat and 0.25 g is trans fat), 17.5 g carbohydrates, 2.0 g sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. A standard 200 g serving (2 pieces) therefore delivers approximately 450 kcal, 800 mg sodium, and a glycemic load of 24.5, making it a high-energy, high-sodium, high-GI food. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high-GI white bread, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory fats and processed meat). Healthier modifications include using wholemeal bread, replacing ham with fresh lean protein, and substituting margarine with a trans-fat-free spread.
common_user_queries: Is roti bakar healthy for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for a ham-and-cheese toasted sandwich on white bread and have been cross-validated. However, the exact filling composition (ham vs. kaya vs. egg) is not specified in the input, and the original data source is a delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary source. Values retained because they align with approved-source benchmarks, but confidence is medium rather than high due to the unverified filling specification and delivery-platform provenance of the original record.
notes: 1. vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for a ham-and-cheese toasted sandwich on white bread; no vitamin-C-containing ingredients are present in the standard preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, potassium, and extended micronutrient data for this specific toastie preparation are not individually reported in the input and could not be confirmed from a single approved-source entry without risk of misattribution; USDA FDC composite sandwich entries provide partial data but not a single exact match for this dish as served in Singapore. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread (GI 70); toasting does not materially alter GI per Atkinson et al. (2008). 4. The input data source is GrabFood (a delivery platform), which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; nutrient values have been retained only because they are consistent with USDA FoodData Central benchmarks for equivalent sandwich compositions. 5. Filling composition assumed to be ham and cheese based on input description and health_suitability reasoning; if the actual filling differs (e.g., kaya and butter only), macros — particularly protein and sodium — would need revision. 6. is_halal set to false because ham (pork) is a standard filling; halal variants exist but cannot be assumed.
